content.full_text:
June 18, 1990

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 803
Sacramento, CA 95804-0807

Re: Question On Ralph M. Brown Act

Dear Sirs:

I am writing you asking advice on an issue where I do not have a clear idea about the proprieties involved.

It has been the practice of the past city councils in Whittier to attend an annual "long-range planning session" with the local Chamber of Commerce. This meeting is held in Palm Springs and consists of a three-day meeting involving planning meetings for future activities of the Chamber of Commerce. The City Council is invited, together with their wives and it has been tradition for the city manager, police chief, and usually at least one other staff member and their spouses to also attend.

This event has not been noticed as a meeting of the Council, and I am concerned that there may be a violation of the Brown Act. I wonder if you have any similar, prior rulings that might help guide me in this situation. It would appear to me that when a quorum of the City Council meets, even in a situation where there are "no actions taken", but with considerable discussion about joint concerns of the Chamber and the city government that this could be a violation of the Brown Act.

I have elected not to attend the session for this reason. However, I would appreciate any guidance that you might furnish me on these types of activities.

One final note, all the expense are paid for by the City of Whittier as respects the council, their wives, and the staff in attendance.

Cordially,

Bob Henderson
Councilman

RLH/my
